title: Judge emits per-dimension binary PASS/FAIL, never a numeric/Likert score
gap: Auditor rubric judge said 'per-dimension scores' — ambiguous, invited a 1-5 scale
guard: A rubric judge decomposes 'is this good?' into one focused binary PASS/FAIL criterion per dimension; a numeric or Likert score is itself a FAIL of the rule. Adjacent scale points are subjective and inconsistent, annotators cluster on the middle to dodge hard calls, and detecting a real difference needs larger samples.
pattern: A judge that grades on a 1-5 scale hides uncertainty in the middle band, produces noisy non-actionable verdicts, and lets a borderline output pass as a '3' instead of forcing a decision.

detector.check: Scan each judge verdict for a numeric/scale token (N/5, N/10, 'score:', a bare integer verdict); require a per-dimension pass|fail enum instead.
detector.signal: A judge verdict carrying a numeric score, or lacking a per-dimension pass/fail.

authority: Hamel Husain & Shreya Shankar, LLM Evals FAQ 2026 (hamel.dev/blog/posts/evals-faq) — binary beats Likert; decompose into focused binary judges
promotion_trigger: First observed instance of a judge emitting a numeric/Likert verdict that gated (passed or failed) real work.
origin: pre-emptive design decision from a 2026-eval-literature review of the Auditor; NOT an observed failure

title: An unvalidated judge is not a gate — verdict counts only past a per-class TNR bar
gap: The auditor's rubric judge was never calibrated against human labels; nothing measured its false-PASS rate
guard: Before a rubric-judge verdict is trusted as a gate, the judge must clear a per-class TNR (true-negative-rate) bar on a small human-labeled slice (PASS + hard-negative FAIL). Aggregate accuracy is not enough: it hides the agreeableness bias where TPR stays high while TNR collapses. Not-yet-validated -> fail closed to a human check or a Do-er re-loop.
pattern: An agreeable judge waves work through: high true-positive rate, collapsed true-negative rate, so aggregate accuracy looks fine while the false-PASS rate is catastrophic and bad work ships silently.

detector.check: failures/tnr.py computes per-dimension TNR = TN/(TN+FP) over a human-labeled calibration slice (positive=PASS); a dimension gates only if TNR >= bar AND it has >= MIN_NEG hard negatives, else not-yet-validated.
detector.signal: A judged dimension gating work with TNR below bar (agreeableness bias: high TPR, collapsed TNR) or with too few hard negatives to measure.

authority: Galileo, Calibrate Your LLM Judge With Human Annotations 2026 (galileo.ai/blog/calibrate-llm-judge-human-annotations)
FutureAGI, LLM-as-Judge Best Practices 2026 (futureagi.com) — agreeableness bias: TPR>96% with TNR<25%
Applied-LLMs / Hamel LLM-as-Judge guide — a judge needs 100+ labeled examples and ongoing maintenance
promotion_trigger: First observed judge false-PASS (a defect shipped that the judge marked pass), or a measured TNR below bar on a real dimension.
origin: pre-emptive design decision from a 2026-eval-literature review of the Auditor; NOT an observed failure

title: Decorrelation is same-family not cross-provider by default; deterministic detectors carry the load
gap: House-rule required a different model, but the Auditor (Sonnet 5) and the Do-er's Opus are the same provider family
guard: Treat same-family model separation (Sonnet 5 vs Opus) as a floor, not the goal: a same-family judge over-rewards its own family's outputs. A cross-provider judge (Gemini/GPT) IS reachable via a shell-out skill but costs an external dependency, so it is declined as default for portability (PD-005) and offered as a capability-gated opt-in. Keep the judge rung thin and let deterministic + structural detectors carry the real decorrelation, since code shares no blind spots with any model. Never let two same-sub-family models (e.g. Opus grading Opus) stand as the audit.
pattern: A judge in the same model family as the builder confidently waves through the exact errors the builder is prone to, because they share blind spots — the audit looks independent but is correlated.

detector.check: Record grader/subject family ids on every judged verdict; while the default judge is same-family, require the gating verdict to rest on deterministic+structural detectors, not the judge alone. A cross-provider opt-in must pass a capability probe (CF-044) and fail closed to Sonnet 5.
detector.signal: A gating decision resting on a same-family judge with no deterministic/structural detector behind it.

authority: FutureAGI / Label Your Data, LLM-as-Judge 2026 — same-family judges over-reward their own family; use a different family (GPT generator -> Claude or Gemini judge)
promotion_trigger: First observed instance of the judge passing an error that the Do-er made and the judge shares (a demonstrated correlated blind spot).
origin: pre-emptive design decision from a 2026-eval-literature review of the Auditor; NOT an observed failure

title: Judge rubrics are versioned like detectors (criteria drift)
gap: Detectors were a versioned SSOT but the judge rubric had no home and no change protocol
guard: Each judged dimension's binary rubric lives in a versioned home beside the detectors, shipped with the calibration slice that validated it. Grading criteria co-evolve with the outputs you see (criteria drift), so a rubric edit is a tracked change that re-runs the TNR gate — never a silent, untracked prompt tweak.
pattern: A judge rubric is edited in place with no version record; a criterion silently shifts, verdicts flip, and no one can tell whether the judge or the work changed.

detector.check: failures/rubrics.py registers each judged dimension's binary rubric as a versioned artifact; content_hash = sha256(criterion|version) must match (silent-edit detector) AND the rubric's calibration dimension must clear the TNR gate (tnr.py).
detector.signal: A rubric whose criterion changed without a version bump / hash re-record, or whose calibration dimension is not TNR-validated.

authority: Shreya Shankar et al., criteria drift & EvalGen (criteria co-evolve with grading)
Eugene Yan, Evaluating LLM-Evaluators (eugeneyan.com/writing/llm-evaluators)
promotion_trigger: First observed verdict flip traced to an untracked rubric edit.
origin: pre-emptive design decision from a 2026-eval-literature review of the Auditor; NOT an observed failure

title: Auditor runs on Claude Sonnet 5, not Fable (portability chosen over cross-provider decorrelation)
gap: The Auditor ran on Fable; a stronger, still-portable judge was wanted without breaking the no-deps guardrail
guard: The Auditor prong runs on Claude Sonnet 5 — a different model from the Opus Do-er (satisfies no-self-grade, CF-059) while keeping Trident zero-dependency and portable. A cross-provider judge (Gemini/GPT) gives stronger family-level decorrelation but needs an external CLI/API/egress dependency; it is declined as the default and offered only as a capability-gated opt-in that fails closed to Sonnet 5. Because the default audit is same-family, deterministic + structural detectors remain the primary decorrelation (PD-003).
pattern: Picking the Auditor model trades three ways: too weak / too correlated a judge misses Do-er errors; too expensive a judge wastes tokens on a deterministic-first prong; a cross-provider judge breaks the no-deps / pure-skill guardrail. Fable was rejected as too weak; Opus-on-Opus rejected as maximally correlated + wasteful.

detector.check: CF-059's grader!=subject!=author model-id equality check enforces Auditor!=Do-er; the trident runbook records Auditor=Sonnet 5. A cross-provider auditor must pass a capability probe (CF-044) and fall back to Sonnet 5 on failure.
detector.signal: An audit verdict whose grader model id equals the Do-er/subject model id, or a cross-provider auditor used with no passing capability probe.

authority: User decision (in-session)
Trident no-deps / pure-skill guardrail (CLAUDE.md hard guardrails)
2026 LLM-as-judge decorrelation finding — different-family ideal, traded off here for portability
promotion_trigger: First observed instance of the Sonnet-5 auditor passing an error the Opus Do-er made and shares (a demonstrated same-family correlated blind spot) — would justify promoting the cross-provider opt-in to default.
origin: design decision recorded in-session; the user chose Sonnet 5 for the Auditor; NOT an observed failure

title: Simba runs on Sonnet 5 (same model as the Auditor) — intake quality over watcher decorrelation
gap: Simba was 'default (cheap)'; a stronger model was wanted for intake extraction and consequence preview, accepting that it now matches the Auditor's model
guard: Simba runs on Claude Sonnet 5, chosen for stronger intake IntentCard extraction (the highest-stakes, error-compounding subtask — every drift check grades against the IntentCard) and the round-1 consequence preview, over a cheaper Haiku. This puts Simba on the SAME model as the Auditor: the no-self-grading invariant still holds (both grade the Opus Do-er's Output, neither grades its own work — CF-059), but the two watcher prongs are now correlated. Mitigations: deterministic + structural detectors remain the primary decorrelation, and Simba reads Output-only (never the Do-er's reasoning) to stay independent of the build.
pattern: Choosing Simba's model trades intake/preview quality against watcher-prong decorrelation: too cheap (Haiku) risks mis-extracting the IntentCard the whole loop grades against; same-as-Auditor (Sonnet) strengthens extraction but correlates the two watchers, so a shared blind spot is missed twice.

detector.check: CF-059 grader!=subject enforces Auditor!=Do-er (Simba is not the final grader). Because Simba shares the Auditor's model, require the drift/conflict verdict to rest on the deterministic tier-1 detector (tests/simba_det_eval.py) before the shared-model semantic residue.
detector.signal: A drift/conflict gating decision resting only on the Sonnet semantic judgment shared by Simba and the Auditor, with no deterministic tier-1 detector behind it.

authority: User decision (in-session)
Simba task-structure analysis: intake IntentCard extraction is error-compounding and highest-stakes; the round-1 consequence preview is forward-reasoning (Haiku's weakest mode)
promotion_trigger: First observed drift that BOTH Simba and the Auditor miss due to a shared-model blind spot (a correlated miss) — would justify splitting Simba to a different model.
origin: design decision recorded in-session; the user chose Sonnet 5 for Simba; NOT an observed failure

title: Simba asks SCOPE first, then intent, before any loop starts; all prong output is nested bullets and tables
gap: Phase 0 spawned Simba with the user's message history and had it INFER the goal. Nothing in the runbook required asking the user. Separately, prong outputs were free-form prose, so structure and attribution were left to the model's discretion. First logged with detector.kind 'deterministic' and no code behind it; the Auditor rejected it under house-rule 11 (no borrowed 'deterministic') and this record was corrected by shipping failures/intent_gate.py rather than downgrading the claim.
guard: Two rules. (15) Simba owns a new step 0 of every Trident session. QUESTION 1 IS ALWAYS "what's the scope for this session?", capturing BOTH in_scope and out_of_scope - a scope with no exclusions is not a scope - asked before the goal question and before any other prong is spawned. Then 2-4 further questions, each a decision that changes what gets built, and build the IntentCard from THEIR ANSWER. Prior messages are corroboration, never the source. Every IntentCard carries intent_source: asked|inferred; 'inferred' is not a valid gate input and the Auditor fails closed on it. Reading an inferred goal back for confirmation does not count as asking - it anchors the user to the guess. Only exception: an explicit user instruction to proceed without asking, recorded verbatim in pinned_feedback. (16) Every Trident-authored surface - orchestrator reports, IntentCard, AssumptionSet, RATVerdict, Verdict, DriftFlag - is nested bullets or tables only. No prose paragraphs. A field needing explanation gets a sub-bullet; comparisons and per-detector results go in a table.
pattern: An inferred goal is the highest-leverage error in the loop, and an unbounded scope is the second: every downstream drift check grades AGAINST the IntentCard, so a wrong goal is never caught later - it is ratified by everything after it. Scope fails the same way but earlier: a correct goal pursued across the wrong surface still burns the session, which is why scope is question 1. Prose output compounds both by hiding structure, letting an unattributed claim ride alongside real findings as if it were one.

detector.check: failures/intent_gate.py (wired into tests/selftest.py, with negative controls that fire): check_intent_source() rejects an IntentCard whose intent_source != 'asked' or is missing, AND whose scope.in_scope or scope.out_of_scope is missing or empty (scope is checked because it is asked first). check_shape() rejects any surface containing a prose paragraph (a run of consecutive non-structural lines totalling >=3 sentences, measured per BLOCK not per line). Both gates check the ARTIFACT only - neither can force Simba to have genuinely asked, so the fields can be stamped untruthfully; that residue is discipline, not a gate.
detector.signal: An IntentCard reaching the Auditor with intent_source missing or 'inferred'; or a prong artifact emitted with a prose paragraph outside a bullet or table.

authority: User instruction (in-session, verbatim): 'Please update trident so that it only writes in nested bullets and tables. Also first ask user intent, never assume it. Give this job to simba for any new trident session'

Corroborating evidence from the same session: Simba's own IntentCard returned determination=ConflictFlag with intent_riskiest naming misread intent as the session's dominant risk, and flagged one user question ('if i choose HTML local in folder, can i still use vibe annotations?') as never confirmed answered - an inferred IntentCard reproducing the gap it was meant to catch
promotion_trigger: First observed session where an asked-and-answered IntentCard still produces a wrong goal - i.e. asking did not prevent the misread - which would show the gate is necessary but not sufficient and needs a second intent source.
origin: design decision recorded in-session at the user's explicit instruction; NOT an observed failure

title: Seed detector: hallucination / unverifiable claim (a-priori, from TraceRoot)
gap: CF detectors are all learned from observed failures; the a-priori 'agent asserts an unverifiable or false claim' mode had no anticipation entry, so a fresh task could hit it with the watch-list blind.
guard: Seed the Auditor's AnticipatedFailures with hallucination/unverifiable-claim. Fail-closed as an llm-judge until it clears tnr.py (PD-002: import TraceRoot's coverage, not its trust model). On promotion prefer the deterministic proxy (CF-061): a factual/result claim with no supporting tool-call or cited source in the same turn.
pattern: An agent states a fact, number, or result it never verified; downstream steps trust it and the error compounds silently, un-caught because nothing anticipated it.

detector.check: Anticipated at intake; on the semantic residue an llm-judge asks per claim, binary: is this supported by a tool result or cited source in the extracted Spans? TNR-gated before it can pass work.
detector.signal: A factual/result claim in the Output with no supporting span or source in the Spans.

authority: TraceRoot Detectors taxonomy (traceroot.ai/docs/getting-started/introduction) — public docs — hallucination detection over agent traces
Trident PD-002 (a judge must clear per-class TNR before it gates)
promotion_trigger: First observed instance of the agent asserting an unverifiable/false claim that gated real work.
origin: pre-emptive: a-priori detector seeded from TraceRoot's taxonomy; NOT an observed failure

title: Seed detector: silent tool-failure (a-priori, from TraceRoot; structurally visible via spans.mjs)
gap: Trident had no anticipation entry for a tool/subprocess call that fails while the agent proceeds as if it succeeded - even though prongs/spans.mjs now makes that structurally visible as a role:'error' span.
guard: Seed AnticipatedFailures with silent tool-failure. prongs/spans.mjs extracts role:'error' spans from the EXECUTED transcript; on promotion wire a structural check that FAILS the Verdict when an error span is unacknowledged/unretried in the Output. Highest reachable rung (CF-061) because the error signal is already executed, not narrated.
pattern: A tool or subprocess returns an error; the agent ignores it and continues, so the final Output rests on a step that never actually succeeded.

detector.check: prongs/spans.mjs surfaces role:'error' (status ERROR) spans; anticipate at intake, and on promotion fail the Verdict when an error span has no corresponding handling in the Output.
detector.signal: An error span (status ERROR) in the Spans with no matching acknowledgement/retry in the Output.

authority: TraceRoot Detectors taxonomy (traceroot.ai/docs/getting-started/introduction) — public docs — tool/logic-failure detection over traces
promotion_trigger: First observed run where a tool/subprocess error was ignored and the Output proceeded as if it had succeeded.
origin: pre-emptive: a-priori detector seeded from TraceRoot's taxonomy; NOT an observed failure

title: Seed detector: logic-error / self-contradiction (a-priori, from TraceRoot)
gap: No anticipation entry for an agent whose result contradicts its own stated plan or an earlier step; caught today only if it happens to trip a learned CF.
guard: Seed AnticipatedFailures with logic-error/self-contradiction. On promotion, deterministic where the contradiction is between two typed artifacts (plan vs result, assumption vs conclusion); llm-judge, TNR-gated, only on prose reasoning residue.
pattern: An agent's output contradicts its own plan, an earlier span, or a stated constraint, and proceeds anyway - an internal inconsistency no single-step check notices.

detector.check: Anticipated at intake as a watch-list mode; promotes to a hybrid detector (typed-artifact diff first, TNR-gated judge on the residue) on first observation.
detector.signal: An Output that asserts X while an earlier span or a stated constraint asserted not-X.

authority: TraceRoot Detectors taxonomy (traceroot.ai/docs/getting-started/introduction) — public docs — logic-error detection over traces
promotion_trigger: First observed run where the Output contradicted its own plan/earlier step and shipped.
origin: pre-emptive: a-priori detector seeded from TraceRoot's taxonomy; NOT an observed failure

title: Seed detector: safety-violation (a-priori, from TraceRoot; reuses the house-rule-6 substrate)
gap: No a-priori anticipation entry for a prohibited or irreversible action taken without approval, beyond the house-rule-6 reversibility field it partly reuses.
guard: Seed AnticipatedFailures with safety-violation. Reuse house-rule 6: an irreversible/prohibited action with no approved_by is the structural core (already enforced by validate_prongs check_rule6_reversibility); an llm-judge, TNR-gated, covers softer policy breaches on promotion.
pattern: An agent performs an irreversible or out-of-policy action (delete, send, publish, mutate) without the approval the reversibility tier requires.

detector.check: Anticipated at intake; the structural gate already exists (check_rule6_reversibility fails an irreversible action with no approved_by). On promotion, extend to a policy-token watch-list plus a TNR-gated judge for softer breaches.
detector.signal: An irreversible/prohibited action in the Output or Spans with no logged approval.

authority: TraceRoot Detectors taxonomy (traceroot.ai/docs/getting-started/introduction) — public docs — safety-violation detection over traces
Trident house-rule 6 (reversibility gate)
promotion_trigger: First observed run where a prohibited/irreversible action was taken without approval and was not caught by the house-rule-6 field.
origin: pre-emptive: a-priori detector seeded from TraceRoot's taxonomy; NOT an observed failure

title: Seed detector: intent-drift (a-priori, from TraceRoot; cross-checked against Simba's DriftFlag)
gap: TraceRoot lists intent-drift as a first-class detector. Trident already has Simba's DriftFlag + tier-1 detector; the question was whether to add a duplicate. Cross-check: it is PARTLY covered, so this seeds the a-priori anticipation rather than a second gate.
guard: Do NOT duplicate Simba. Simba's tier-1 detector plus the DriftFlag.determination enum already own the drift RULING; this seed only widens the Auditor's AnticipatedFailures so Simba is primed to watch for drift on tasks a-priori prone to it (long multi-turn, scope-heavy), before it fires. If a real drift escapes Simba, promote to a CF that hardens Simba's detector, not a parallel one.
pattern: Across a long or multi-surface task the agent gradually optimizes for something other than the stated goal/scope; each step looks locally fine, so drift is only visible against the original IntentCard.

detector.check: Anticipated at intake and folded into Simba's watch-list; the RULING stays with Simba's existing tier-1 detector + DriftFlag.determination (no second gate). Promotion hardens Simba's detector, never adds a duplicate.
detector.signal: A drift Simba's current detector misses that the a-priori anticipation would have flagged.

authority: TraceRoot Detectors taxonomy (traceroot.ai/docs/getting-started/introduction) — public docs — intent-drift detection over traces
Trident PD-006 / Simba DriftFlag (existing drift ruling)
promotion_trigger: First observed intent-drift that Simba's current detector missed - which would justify hardening Simba's detector (not adding a parallel one).
origin: pre-emptive: a-priori detector seeded from TraceRoot's taxonomy; NOT an observed failure

title: Outward-impact ratchet: Trident tracks real-run audit coverage + census non-regression as a forward-only gate
gap: Trident had near-maxed its INTERNAL metrics (14/14 mutation, ~60 controls, green selftest) while the OUTWARD metric it exists for - defects caught across real Do-er runs - had n~4 and was measured by nothing. census half-admitted this ('fixtures passing is not coverage') but only counted transport/enforcement gaps, non-blocking, so the 2 open gaps could silently grow and every tick still print green - the same Goodhart, one level up. The original plan promoted a north-star prevention_integrity keyed on 'escapes' (a false-PASS proxy); the Auditor REJECTED escapes/localized/prevention_integrity as not deterministically computable from the current schema (override targets a probe not a detector; no per-detector resolution status; no supersede link) and showed escapes would misclassify c0-audit's knowingly-approved fails. This records the surviving COMPUTABLE subset.
guard: A RATCHET, not a threshold. tests/impact.py is a scoreboard by default and, under --strict, exits 1 if ANY headline number regresses vs the committed tests/impact-baseline.json: outward metrics (runs_genuine, runs_audited, audit_rate=lead, self_heal) may not fall; recurrences (CF-scoped) and every census gap count (transport/exchange/enforcement/unlinked) may not rise. One baseline, one rule ('no headline number regresses'), no absolute bar (no gate-fatigue), nothing can silently rot. Raising the baseline is an explicit logged act (impact.py --set-baseline), never silent - the forward-only ethos applied to every tracked number. escapes/localized/prevention_integrity are DEFERRED to Track B behind a per-detector disposition:accepted|unresolved|superseded field; only then does 'escape'='closed with an unresolved failing detector' become a real computation and prevention_integrity can replace audit_rate as the lead.
pattern: A number nobody watches decays to a vanity light: census gaps were pure backlog, so a rise from 2 to 5 would still print green. The failure mode of a fix is to promote a metric that cannot be measured truthfully - a north-star computed from an absent field takes the shape of the nearest acceptable value (CF-065 vacuous pass, one level up), which IS the vanity metric the gate claims to kill. The ratchet answers both: it enforces a trend on only what the schema can compute, and refuses to name a north-star the ledger cannot yet support.

detector.check: tests/impact.py (wired into tests/selftest.py as a blocking _run with --strict, with negative controls that fire): computes runs_genuine/runs_audited/audit_rate/self_heal/recurrences over census.is_genuine_run runs (reused, not duplicated) plus the census gap feed parsed from census.py as a subprocess; regressions() flags any headline number that moved the wrong way vs impact-baseline.json and --strict exits 1. 12 controls (gut a metric, a control flips) via impact.py --controls, and tests/test-impact.mjs proves the ratchet turns red when a genuine run is dropped or an unaudited run drags audit_rate down, and stays a scoreboard without --strict. The gate reads the ARTIFACTS only - it cannot force an audit to actually happen, so audit_rate can be gamed by stamping empty verdicts; that residue is Track B (disposition), not this gate.
detector.signal: A headline number regressed vs impact-baseline.json with no explicit --set-baseline (outward metric fell, or a census gap rose); or a north-star was promoted while its inputs are uncomputable from the schema.

authority: User instruction (in-session): approved the Auditor's split verdict ('Run A, then discuss what could be an elegant way to solve B') - build the computable Track A ratchet now, defer the north-star.

Auditor verdict (deterministic detectors first): D3 FAILED - escapes NOT-COMPUTABLE (override->probe not detector; no per-detector resolution status; no supersede field) and false-positives on c0-audit's ruling:APPROVED non-blocking fails; so escapes/localized/prevention_integrity are deferred, audit_rate is the interim lead.
promotion_trigger: First genuine run where audit_rate falls because a real defect escaped an unaudited close - OR the per-detector disposition field lands (Track B), at which point escapes becomes computable and prevention_integrity replaces audit_rate as the lead (log that schema change as its own PD).
origin: design decision recorded in-session; the surviving computable subset (Track A) of an Auditor-rejected plan whose north-star was uncomputable

title: Orchestrator pre-runs the deterministic tools ONCE and injects their OUTPUT into prong prompts; prongs invoke, they do not re-derive by reading source
gap: A prong prompt that hands a subagent a task but not the already-computed facts forces the subagent to re-derive the repo's structure by reading source (grep/read sweeps to rediscover ids, schemas, check signatures). That re-derivation is a pure token leak: the orchestrator already ran validate_prongs, mutate, git and grep, so every fact was in hand and got thrown away and recomputed inside the expensive prong. Nothing made the leak visible, so it recurred silently every dispatch.
guard: The orchestrator runs the existing deterministic tools (prongs/validate_prongs.py, prongs/mutate.py) plus git/grep ONCE at intake and INJECTS their captured output as facts into the prong prompt. The prong INVOKES those tools to act; it does not re-derive structural facts by reading source. A prong that re-reads source to rediscover what was already injected is doing the leak this decision names. The injected-facts block is the contract; reading a file is permitted only to fetch an exact string to edit, never to re-establish a fact already provided.
pattern: Handing a subagent the goal without the ground truth turns every dispatch into a fresh exploration of the same repo. The cost scales with repo size and dispatch count, and it is invisible from the output (the answer looks the same whether it was injected or re-derived) — which is exactly why it needs a detector that reads the trace, not the prose.

detector.check: prongs/validate_prongs.py check_claim_has_span(spans) (PD-008/CF-072): a prong that re-derived facts by reading source leaves read/grep spans; a prong that invoked the injected tools leaves the tool-call spans. The floor case — a verdict with NO executed tool-call span at all — is a claim narrated with zero evidence and FAILS. Re-derivation is thereby visible in the extracted Spans, not hidden in the narration.
detector.signal: A prong's Spans show source-reading sweeps to rediscover facts the orchestrator already injected, or (floor case CF-072) a verdict/claim turn with zero executed tool-call spans.

authority: TraceRoot Detectors taxonomy (traceroot.ai/docs/getting-started/introduction) — public docs — trace-level observability of what an agent actually did vs narrated
Trident detectors-seed.md PD-008 (hallucination / unverifiable-claim, deterministic proxy: a claim with no tool-call span in the same turn)
promotion_trigger: First dispatch whose extracted Spans show a prong re-deriving injected facts by reading source rather than invoking the pre-run tools — promote to a CF with a tighter per-fact re-derivation detector.
origin: design decision recorded in-session (MOVE-1): the token-leak fix that motivated building the spans-based claim-has-span detector (check_claim_has_span in prongs/validate_prongs.py, TARGETS-covered in prongs/mutate.py)

title: A build phase must record an in-repo reuse scan (prior_art.reuse) before its RAT opens — reuse > buy > build
gap: The buy-vs-build check surfaced real value once (adopt halo-record instead of hand-rolling a hash-chain) but it ran ad hoc, by hand, only because someone thought to ask. Nothing forced 'does this capability already exist in THIS repo?' before a build phase opened, so the default path was always build — and the cheapest capability, one already sitting in the repo, is the one most easily overlooked and silently rebuilt.
guard: Before a build RATVerdict opens, the orchestrator runs an in-repo reuse scan (grep for the capability) and records the result on the RATVerdict as prior_art.reuse. Ordering is reuse > buy > build: buy-search is triage-gated, reached for only when reuse finds nothing and the build is non-trivial. check_prior_art forward-gates the ledger: a ratverdict dated >= the cutoff with no non-empty prior_art.reuse fails, so a phase cannot open without the scan on record. Existing ratverdicts are grandfathered (forward-only).
pattern: Without a reuse-first gate every phase silently defaults to build; prior art in the same repo is invisible from the goal statement, so the token cost of rebuilding it is paid over and over with nothing making the waste visible. A deterministic gate that reads the RATVerdict, not the prose, makes 'did we check?' answerable.

detector.check: prongs/validate_prongs.py check_prior_art(rows): a ratverdict with ts >= PRIOR_ART_FROM must carry prior_art.reuse (non-empty). TARGETS-covered in prongs/mutate.py (killed by its PD-015 control).
detector.signal: A post-cutoff ratverdict row opening a build phase with no prior_art.reuse field, or an empty one.

authority: In-session buy-vs-build finding (adopt halo-record for hash-chain integrity rather than hand-roll it), generalized to reuse-first
Least-effort / DRY: the cheapest correct capability is one that already exists — check the repo before the market before the keyboard
promotion_trigger: First build phase that rebuilt a capability already present in-repo despite the gate (prior_art.reuse recorded but wrong) — promote to a keyword-matched reuse-scan detector that greps for the named capability itself.
origin: design decision recorded in-session: the reuse-scan pre-RAT gate (task #3 of the open-threads sequence), the durable form of the buy-vs-build finding the user asked to keep firing before every build.

title: Design/reasoning prongs are read-only and SPECIFY probes; only the orchestrator or a build prong EXECUTES them
gap: The forgery probe that killed S1-as-scoped cost 82k tokens because the Auditor — a reasoning prong — RAN it, when it should have been a ~2k orchestrator bash call. Capability (the power to execute) and reasoning (the job of deciding) were conflated, so an expensive reasoning model did cheap mechanical work and the token cost of a single probe was unbounded.
guard: Design/reasoning prongs (ratverdict/intent/assumptions from Simba and the Auditor) are spawned read-only — no Bash — and emit a probe SPEC. The orchestrator (or a dedicated build prong with execution) runs the probe. check_design_prong_no_execution backstops the discipline: a design artifact whose extracted spans include an execution (Bash/shell) span fails, because it means the reasoning prong executed instead of specifying. Scoped to design kinds so build prongs (verdict) that legitimately execute are exempt.
pattern: Conflating capability with reasoning lets expensive reasoning tokens absorb cheap mechanical work; separating the two — the orchestrator owns execution, the prong owns the decision — caps the token cost of any single probe at the orchestrator's bash call, not the prong's reasoning budget. This is least-privilege applied to token economics.

detector.check: prongs/validate_prongs.py check_design_prong_no_execution(spans, kind): for a design kind (ratverdict/intent/assumptions), any execution (Bash/shell) span in the transcript fails. Reuses spans.mjs extraction. TARGETS-covered in prongs/mutate.py (killed by its PD-016 control).
detector.signal: A ratverdict/intent/assumptions transcript whose extracted spans include a Bash/shell execution span — the design prong ran a probe instead of specifying it.

authority: Capability separation / least privilege (a component gets only the powers its role needs) — OS security principle applied to agent orchestration
In-session 82k-forgery RCA: 'forgery itself should have been my 2k bash call, not the Auditor's 82k'
promotion_trigger: First design prong caught at dispatch with execution spans — promote from a post-hoc ledger backstop to a spawn-time capability restriction (deny the Bash tool when dispatching a design prong).
origin: design decision recorded in-session: capability separation (task #2 of the open-threads sequence), the durable fix for the 82k-forgery-in-a-prong token leak the user flagged.

title: Every phase that shipped a guard closes with prove-durable (a durability record) — the conditional bookend to HR-0's phase-open RAT
gap: HR-0 makes every phase OPEN with a RAT, but nothing made a phase CLOSE by proving its fix durable. A fix that adds a guard can ship with the guard un-proven — and we hit exactly that: the first census durability fix was ungated, the guard reverted along with the fix, and the baseline hit sys.exit and went silently green. Mutation testing proves a checker is killed by its own control (durability at rest), but it never reverts the ACTUAL fix in the ACTUAL file to confirm the real suite flips — so the fix-and-guard-revert-together leak had no gate.
guard: A phase that shipped a guard — operationalized as a verdict that `resolves` a CF (a real fix, which by Trident discipline becomes a check) — must also carry a `durability` record before it closes: verdictId links the resolving verdict, `reverted` names the fix that was undone, `control_flipped` names the control that went RED once the fix was gone (prove-durable). check_durability_gate rejects a resolves-verdict with no matching durability record. Conditional by construction: it keys off `resolves`, so RAT/audit phases that shipped no fix are exempt (no-op ban) — the gate never fires an expensive revert on a phase with nothing to prove. Pre-emptive guards (PDs) are exempt because mutation already covers the checker-durability class; this gate covers the fix-driven class mutation cannot. Forward-only from the cutoff so existing resolved verdicts are grandfathered.
pattern: A fix and the guard that protects it can be reverted together, leaving a suite that is green because the thing it would catch is also gone. Durability is only real when you undo the fix and watch a named control go red. Scoping the proof to guard-shipping phases makes it a bookend to HR-0 (open with the riskiest assumption, close with proof the fix holds) without imposing ceremony on phases that produced no fix.

detector.check: prongs/validate_prongs.py check_durability_gate(rows): a verdict with a non-empty `resolves` and ts >= DURABILITY_FROM must have a `durability` row whose verdictId matches and whose `reverted` and `control_flipped` are non-empty. Schema declares the `durability` kind + required fields (guarded by check_schema_kinds / SCHEMA_DEPENDENCIES). TARGETS-covered in prongs/mutate.py (killed by its PD-017 control).
detector.signal: A post-cutoff verdict that resolves a CF (ships a guard) with no matching durability record naming the reverted fix and the control that flipped.

authority: Trident prove-durable discipline (reversion testing): a fix is durable only if reverting it flips a control red
In-session census incident: the ungated durability fix whose guard reverted with the fix and went silently green (the failure this generalizes)
promotion_trigger: First guard-shipping phase whose durability record names a control that does NOT actually flip on revert (a forged proof) — promote from a presence gate to a check that re-runs the revert and confirms the named control fails.
origin: design decision recorded in-session: user asked to add /prove-durable to the end of each phase; after debate, scoped to guard-producing phases and enforced deterministically as the conditional phase-close bookend to HR-0 (rather than an unconditional per-phase ritual, which the no-op ban forbids).

title: RCA is COMPULSORY on any fail — a deterministic gate, not an optional doctrine: every failing detector needs an rca (diagnosis) or an override (acceptance)
gap: RCA-on-fail was already prescribed in the runbook (SKILL.md step 9) and check_rca validated an rca IF one existed — but nothing COMPELLED an rca when a detector failed. A rule written in a SKILL file is a reminder; only an executed check is a gate. So under momentum the Do-er (and the operator) could jump straight from a fail to a symptom-patch — fix this passage, add this filter — with no recorded root cause. The whole class of 'patched the symptom three times instead of diagnosing the substrate' lived in this gap: the diagnosis step existed but was skippable.
guard: check_rca_on_fail forward-gates the ledger: a verdict carrying a failing detector (result=fail), dated >= cutoff, must be met by EITHER an rca grounded in that verdict (rca.verdictId == verdict.id — root cause + fix_hypothesis, validated by check_rca) OR an override that explicitly accepts each failing detector (override.overrides == verdict.id AND override.detector_id == detector). Neither present = an un-diagnosed fail and the ledger is rejected. RCA becomes compulsory on failure; the operator must diagnose the cause (or explicitly accept it) before any fix. Overridden detectors are a reasoned exception, not an un-diagnosed fail.
pattern: A diagnosis step that is optional is a diagnosis step that gets skipped when there is momentum to ship a fix. Making RCA a deterministic trigger on every fail forces the question 'what is the ROOT cause?' before a patch can stand in for it — the difference between fixing the symptom (this retrieval hit the TOC -> add a digit filter) and the diagnosis (retrieval runs on an unstructured blob -> build a structured index).

detector.check: prongs/validate_prongs.py check_rca_on_fail(rows): a post-cutoff verdict with >=1 failing detector must have a matching rca (by verdictId) OR every failing detector overridden (by overrides+detector_id). TARGETS-covered in prongs/mutate.py (killed by its PD-018 control).
detector.signal: A verdict with a result=fail detector and neither an rca grounded in it nor an override accepting the failing detector.

authority: Trident house-rules: 'a rule in a SKILL file is a reminder; only an executed check is a gate'
In-session operator failure: patched the retrieval symptom (TOC-skip, digit-density) three times instead of diagnosing the substrate (unstructured blob) — the RCA that was never run
promotion_trigger: First fail whose rca is present but names a symptom-level root_cause (e.g. targets the passage, not the substrate) — promote to a check that scores the rca's root_cause against the failing_span's component, rejecting symptom-level diagnoses.
origin: design decision recorded in-session: user asked why the available Auditor RCA was not used and how to make it a deterministic trigger on every fail so the diagnosis (not the symptom) gets fixed. The doctrine existed in the runbook; this makes it an enforced gate.

title: AI-Native Spec Contract — the spec-of-spec: a rich, gate-able rubric an AI-native handoff must satisfy (6 build dims + an ownership axis), acceptance EARNED from observed failures, production-readiness ENGINEER-OWNED
gap: Trident had no defined standard for what an AI-native handoff must contain. The field's default (a 4-pillar prototype/prompt/state/acceptance model) covers ~1 of the ML Test Score's 4 production-readiness categories, narrows 'interface' to the prompt (hiding the API and data contracts engineers integrate against), and asserts an acceptance number upfront instead of earning it from observed failures. Worse, it lets a PM COMPLETE the whole artifact — which in the engineer's lens is information leakage + overexposure, and removes the engineering ownership of reliability that builds trust. With no typed home the standard would decay to prose (the prongs/schema.json law).
guard: A reference doc (.claude/skills/references/ai-native-spec.md) defines the AI-Native Spec Contract: 6 build dimensions (prototype-as-spec with real model calls; interface contracts split into model+API+data; failure&fallback; eval contract; observability; versioning&regression) + a cross-cutting ownership&socialization axis. Two MUSTs: (1) EARNED acceptance — the eval contract must cite >=1 executed iteration over a real dataset whose failure taxonomy traces to observed spans; zero iterations => incomplete. (2) OPEN ROOM not completion — every production-readiness dimension names an engineering owner and leaves open room; a PM-closed dimension with no named owner fails (the trust check; no human sign-off gate, ownership transfers demo-driven and contestable). Score-loop weights dim 4 (eval) highest. Engineer's lens is graded against A Philosophy of Software Design (deep vs shallow module, information hiding vs leakage, define-errors-out-of-existence, design-it-twice), and the handoff is organized by owner/information — never by phase chronology (temporal decomposition).
pattern: An AI-native handoff shipped as authored PM claims — a resemblance/screenshot spec — with no earned eval and no engineering ownership: it passes on vibes and fails in production, and the PM 'did it all', destroying trust. Or the standard exists only as prose in a SKILL file and decays to zero use.

detector.check: This session ships the rubric as a documented standard only (reminder-tier, house-rules rung 1). The deterministic form is specified and sequenced downstream (after the phase-model DAG restructure): check_handoff_completeness (a close with no earned eval iteration is rejected), check_ownership_room (a production-readiness dimension with no named engineering owner / open_room is rejected), check_contract (model+API+data sub-contracts present), check_fallback (a degradation path present). NARRATED != EXECUTED: those validators do not exist yet and this record does not claim they do.
detector.signal: A handoff whose eval contract cites no executed iteration over a real dataset (an asserted acceptance number), or a production-readiness dimension with no named engineering owner / open room, or an interface contract that is only a prompt contract (no API/data sub-contract).

authority: A Philosophy of Software Design (Ousterhout) — the engineer's lens: deep modules, information hiding vs leakage, define errors out of existence, design it twice, the red-flag list (a handoff is graded as a design artifact)

The ML Test Score (Breck et al., Google, 2017) — a 4-category production-readiness rubric (Features/Data, Model Development, Infrastructure, Monitoring); the common 4-pillar handoff covers ~one category
Husain & Shankar, LLM Evals (2026) — acceptance is earned from observed failures via error analysis on real traces, not asserted upfront
GitHub Spec Kit (2025) — spec-driven development: the spec generates the implementation
promotion_trigger: First AI-native handoff produced under Trident whose eval contract cites no executed observed-failure iteration, OR whose production-readiness dimension has no named engineering owner — promote to a real CF and ship the deterministic check_handoff_completeness / check_ownership_room gates (the downstream spec-machinery plan).
origin: design decision recorded in-session: the operator re-scoped Trident to produce AI-native handoffs. The spec-of-spec rubric was derived, critiqued against the field's 4-pillar model, grounded in public authorities (ML Test Score, eval-driven-development, Spec Kit, A Philosophy of Software Design), and audited by the user-loyal prong for intent-fit. Pre-emptive: the enforcing validators are sequenced downstream after the phase-model DAG restructure; this session ships the standard + its typed home so it does not decay.

title: Human-in-loop observability + checkpoint/interrupt layer for the agent loop (Claude-Code-native), conforming to LangGraph HITL + OTel GenAI + Anthropic trustworthy-agents
gap: A loop (eval->deficiency->retrieve->fix->re-eval) that runs to convergence gives the human no observability into what it did each iteration and no control points to approve/edit/reject/rollback. The human cannot feel in command, and there is no durable, standard-conforming record of the loop's decisions.
guard: prongs/checkpoint.mjs writes a resumable CHECKPOINT record per iteration to runs/<runId>/checkpoints.jsonl. Observability is CONTINUOUS (a checkpoint every round, carrying the executed spans from spans.mjs); control is DISCRETE (write-checkpoint+exit-3 = interrupt at consequential/irreversible steps; resume via approve/edit/reject/rollback). Nothing auto-applies (propose != dispose). Every checkpoint field cites its authority inline; the schema is prongs/checkpoint.schema.json.
pattern: A loop without a per-iteration checkpoint + interrupt contract is a black box: the human sees only the final output, cannot steer mid-flight, and has no standard-shaped trace. The fix is the LangGraph checkpoint/interrupt/resume loop, instrumented with OTel-GenAI-shaped spans, under Anthropic's keep-humans-in-control invariants.

detector.check: prongs/checkpoint.schema.json validates each checkpoint record; a promote/irreversible op must be preceded by an approved interrupt (pause-before-irreversible gate, to be added to validate_prongs.py). checkpoint.mjs write/resume/show tested end-to-end (interrupt exits 3; resume threads parent).
detector.signal: A loop that applies an irreversible change with no interrupted checkpoint the human approved; or a checkpoint missing the required OTel/LangGraph fields.

authority.grounds: Control contract: checkpoint every step (resumable state) + interrupt() to pause + approve/edit/reject/respond decision types + resume via Command + time-travel/rollback. Realized in checkpoint.mjs as write-checkpoint+exit-3 (interrupt), resume --approve/--edit/--reject/--rollback, and the `parent` pointer (rollback).

Observability shape: capture the DECISION GRAPH as spans with a gen_ai.operation.name, not just I/O. Realized as the checkpoint's `gen_ai.operation.name` + `spans[]` populated by prongs/spans.mjs (reduced OpenInference).

authority.source: Anthropic - trustworthy agents / building effective agents
authority.grounds: Invariants: keep humans in control, transparency (show the plan), checkpoint before irreversible actions, explicit stopping conditions, ask when uncertain. Realized as the human-readable card (transparency), the stop{} block, the pause-before-irreversible interrupt on a `promote` op, and propose!=dispose (human disposes on resume).

promotion_trigger: First loop run where an irreversible change lands with no approved interrupt checkpoint -- promote the pause-before-irreversible check from advisory to a hard validate_prongs gate.
origin: design decision recorded in-session: build a best-in-class human-in-loop observability layer in Trident (always available), conforming to the authoritative HITL/observability standards; the authority provenance itself is made durable via this structured authority field (source/url/accessed/grounds) so it is never lost.
